Home › Calculators › Hourly to Salary Calculator

📆 Hourly to Salary Calculator

Comparing an hourly job to a salaried offer? Convert your hourly wage into annual terms. Enter your rate, weekly hours and weeks worked per year to see the equivalent salary — before and adjusted for time off.

The conversion, and the quick trick

Annual salary = hourly rate × hours per week × weeks worked. The handy shortcut for a standard full-time year (40 hours × ~50 weeks = 2,000 hours) is to multiply your hourly rate by 2,000: $25/hour ≈ $50,000/year. In reverse, divide a salary by 2,000 to estimate the hourly equivalent — a $60,000 salary is about $30/hour.

Why "weeks worked" matters

Hourly workers are usually paid only for hours actually worked, so unpaid time off directly reduces annual income. Salaried employees are typically paid across all 52 weeks including vacation. That difference — captured by the "weeks worked" field — is a real gap when comparing an hourly role to a salary. The "if paid all 52 weeks" line shows the ceiling if every week were compensated.

Comparing offers fairly

  • Benefits: salaried roles often include paid leave, health coverage, and retirement matching that hourly roles may not. Add their value before comparing.
  • Overtime: hourly work may pay 1.5× beyond 40 hours — a premium salaried staff rarely get.
  • Stability: salary smooths income across slow periods; hourly pay swings with available hours.

All figures here are gross (pre-tax). Your take-home depends on your tax situation — use a take-home pay estimator for the net comparison that actually hits your bank account.

Frequently asked questions

How many hours are in a work year?

A standard full-time year is about 2,080 hours (40 × 52) before time off, or roughly 2,000 working hours after typical vacation. The '× 2000' shortcut uses the latter for quick mental conversion.

Should I compare hourly and salary jobs on gross pay?

Start with gross for an apples-to-apples base, then adjust for benefits, paid leave, overtime potential, and stability, which often differ significantly between hourly and salaried roles.

Does this account for taxes?

No — it shows gross income. Net take-home depends on your country, filing status, and deductions; use a take-home pay calculator for the after-tax figure.

Further reading

"Understanding Your Paycheck: Where Your Money Actually Goes" A line-by-line guide to your pay stub — gross vs net, taxes, and deductions — so you know exactly why your take-home is less than your salary. "ETFs vs Mutual Funds: Which Should You Choose?" The real differences between ETFs and mutual funds — trading, fees, taxes and minimums — and why for most long-term investors it barely matters which you pick. "Changing Jobs? The Four Things You Can Do With Your Old 401(k)" Leave it, roll it to the new plan, roll it to an IRA, or cash out — the real trade-offs of each, the direct-rollover rule that avoids a tax disaster, and who should pick which. "Renting vs Buying a Home: The Honest Math" Beyond 'rent is throwing money away' — the real costs on both sides, the break-even horizon, and how to decide based on your actual situation.

This calculator is for educational purposes only and does not constitute financial advice. Results are estimates based on the inputs and assumptions shown.
